Finanace Simulator

Monte Carlo analysis of returns, inflation, and dynamic flows.

Sequence of Returns

Market returns and inflation are based on historical data. Everything in this tool — portfolio balances, withdrawals, and reported market returns — is in real (today’s) dollars and real returns, so you don’t need to factor inflation in yourself. Withdrawals include taxes: capital gains and income taxes are assumed to come out of the withdrawal, so the net amount you can spend is lower. Other income such as a pension or Social Security is not modeled.

Use a range of years around the endpoint. Leave both at 0 for a fixed timeline.

000s
ConservativeBalancedAggressive

Find Best Plan

Search for the highest sustainable spending plan instead of simulating fixed numbers. Check Include in search on settings below to choose what Find Best Plan can tune.

Minimum acceptable chance the plan hits both targets below.

Max lifetime spending shortfall vs. plan in bad markets. 0% = stay on plan; higher = bigger plan, more belt-tightening allowed. Minimum withdrawals still limit how far any single year can be cut.

000s

Balance to leave behind at the end of the horizon, on top of never running out.

Withdrawal Strategy
000s

In today's dollars — the simulator keeps purchasing power constant, so don't build in inflation raises.

Spending Over Time

Stage how your base withdrawal changes over retirement. Each tier's annual real change compounds continuously (negative = spend more now, less later); extra withdrawal adds a flat amount on top for that tier's years. Set any value to 0. Add tiers to stage different patterns — the last tier applies to all remaining years. Remove all but one tier for a flat schedule. When Find Best Plan includes this section, it tunes only the first tier's extra withdrawal.

Major Events

Model expected cash events outside your spending schedule — selling a house or business, inheritance, reverse-mortgage draws, or known large payments. Positive amounts add to the portfolio; negative amounts are extra payments on top of that year's plan. Start year is year 1 of retirement. Leave Years blank for a one-time event, or enter how many consecutive years it repeats. Not used with a Specific List — bake those into your list instead. Events appear as dots on the schedule chart below.

Minimum withdrawal

Limits how far guardrails can cut your plan each year (capped by portfolio balance) — your schedule above is the plan; the chart below shows the plan line and this floor separately. Add tiers to stage different minimums over time — the last tier applies to all remaining years. Remove all tiers for no minimum. Doesn't apply to a Specific List.

Years in a row at the minimum before returning to plan. 0 = off.

yrs

Spend at least the plan for this many years after the streak. 0 = off.

Paste from a spreadsheet — new lines, tabs, commas, semicolons, or pipes. Negative values are deposits. Last value is reused for remaining years.

Minimum withdrawal

Limits how far market/balance adjustments can cut each year's list amount (capped by portfolio balance) — the list above is the plan. Add tiers to stage different minimums over time — the last tier applies to all remaining years. Remove all tiers for no minimum.

Years in a row at the minimum before returning to plan. 0 = off.

yrs

Spend at least the plan for this many years after the streak. 0 = off.

Gifting

Add an extra gift withdrawal in years when your portfolio balance (after growth and your regular withdrawal or deposit) exceeds the tier's balance threshold and that year's spending fully met your plan — including any glide-path surplus, so a temporary market cut that glide replaces does not block the gift. Add tiers to stage different gifts over time — the last tier applies to all remaining years. Set Gift to 0 (or leave Balance blank) on early tiers to defer gifting to later years. Remove all tiers for no gifting. The chart below shows a dotted gift ceiling above your planned withdrawal.

Dynamic Adjustments & Guardrails

Market adjustment

Adjust withdrawal based on real market return (see chart). At the Expected return the plan is on track, so its adjustment is normally 0. When the balance is above the No cut threshold, downward adjustments are skipped — a bad market year never cuts spending while the portfolio is still ahead. Max boost drawdown limits how large a high-market boost can be so the balance after that year’s spending (except glide) does not fall more than this percent below the year’s starting balance — negative values require growth instead (e.g. −1% = finish at least 1% above start). Blank = no limit; only the market boost is reduced, never your base plan.

000s
blank = off

Balance adjustment

Smoothly scale your total withdrawal based on portfolio balance: below the floor, spending ramps down toward the max cut (reached at $0); above the ceiling, it ramps up by the boost rate for every extra multiple of the ceiling — with no upper cap. Between the two, spending is unchanged. When Find Best Plan includes this section, it pins the floor to the lowest balance band and the ceiling to the highest, then tunes max cut and boost rate (never 0%).

Floor Balance Max Cut
000s
%

0 = no floor

Ceiling Balance Boost Rate
000s
%

0 = no ceiling

Glide-path spend-down

Actively spend surplus down toward a target ending balance. Each year the simulator computes the balance still needed to fund your remaining plan and land on the target at the horizon; after any eligible gift is paid, when the portfolio still sits above that glide path, the spend rate share of the remaining surplus is withdrawn on top of the plan. The glide path declines toward the target as the horizon shrinks, so extra spending concentrates in the later years of comfortable paths and never cuts below your plan. Slide spend timing toward later to hold surplus back for late retirement — it raises the glide path so the early years stay invested, which helps plans that would otherwise dip to their minimum withdrawals mid-retirement. When Find Best Plan includes this section, it sets the target from Find Best Plan's Target Ending Balance and tunes the spend rate.

000s

blank = off; 0 = spend to zero

%

share of surplus spent each year

later balanced sooner

Explore specific paths

A 3D plot of representative paths from the 5th to 65th percentiles. Column height represents portfolio balance, color reflects the annual real market return for that year (Red = crash, Green = boom). Drag to rotate, scroll to zoom, right-drag to pan. Single-click a column to pin that simulation and mouse over each year for values; click the column again or empty space to release. Double-click a column to explore ~200 nearby simulations; double-click again to return.

Sequence Risk: IRR vs Average Return

Each dot is one simulation. Dots below the dashed diagonal were hurt by early bad years — their money-weighted IRR trails their time-weighted average return; dots above it got lucky early. Paths in the lower-right had good average returns but still fell short of the plan: sequence risk in its purest form. Click a dot to see that path's withdrawals against its plan.

Average Timelines

Shows portfolio balance and dynamic withdrawals over time for the 65th through 5th percentile paths. Point size and shape reflect each year's real return.

Distribution of Real Returns

Summary of average annual real return across all simulations. Each stat also shows the IRR — the money-weighted return, which accounts for the timing of withdrawals and deposits.

Advanced simulation settings

How many CPU cores to use when running simulations. Higher is faster but uses more of your computer while the run is in progress.

%

Averages a band of runs around each percentile (P5–P65) to reduce noise. 0 = a single run.

Primary metric for ranking (P5–P65), on-plan success, 3D coloring, and Find Best Plan. Auto picks mean/yr (total ÷ years lived) when a horizon range is set. Total withdrawn is still shown alongside results.

Max spending shortfall vs. plan when scoring runs. Affects the “on plan” success rate and 3D chart coloring. Find Best Plan uses its own Risk Tolerance when enabled.

Adds year-to-year persistence by drawing runs of consecutive years averaging this length (Historical Resampling and Smoothed Historical); set to 1 for fully independent years. Log-Normal uses it for year-to-year smoothing instead.

Softens Smoothed Historical's output so it isn't limited to only the exact years in your selected range. Not used by the other two methods.

How many simulations Find Best Plan runs to score each candidate plan while searching. Lower = faster search but noisier results; higher = slower but more reliable. The final plan is always confirmed using the full Number of Simulations above.
